comment_content:
This is an excellent, low-pressure course designed specifically for individuals with prior programming experience who need to quickly get up to speed with Python. It successfully serves its purpose as a bridging or starter course, providing a smooth transition into the language without overwhelming the student.

The content is well-structured, progressing logically from fundamental programming concepts (like variables, functions, and control loops) to more advanced Python-specific topics. The curriculum doesn't waste time on absolute programming basics, instead focusing on how to apply existing programming knowledge within Python's syntax and paradigms. The guided programming exercises are particularly effective, reinforcing the self-learned material from the provided resources.
comment_teaching: The purely online format is executed very well. Course materials are clearly organized and accessible, allowing for self-paced learning. The structure supports independent study while providing the necessary guidance through exercises and, presumably, instructor availability for questions. It's an ideal setup for someone who is disciplined and prefers flexibility.
comment_workload: The workload is accurately described as light. The requirement of approximately one lab or exercise set per week is very manageable, even for students balancing other commitments. This makes it possible to engage meaningfully with the material without it becoming a significant source of stress.
comment_grading: The Pass/Fail grading scheme is one of the course's best features. It removes the anxiety associated with letter grades and allows students to focus purely on learning the material. As noted, the grading is fair and the course is designed for success; anyone who engages consistently with the weekly exercises is virtually assured of passing. This creates a supportive and positive learning environment.

comment_content: The course covers the fundamentals of Python programming in a step-by-step manner. Topics include variables, lists and strings, loops (for and while), functions, recursion, file input/output, and an introduction to object-oriented programming. There are also some small projects like Conway’s Game of Life and using turtle graphics to draw trees, which make the material a bit more engaging compared to just reading.
comment_teaching: Since this is an online bridging course, there are no traditional lectures. All the material is provided on the CSE website, and students are expected to go through it independently. The explanations are simple and direct, so beginners should be able to follow without too much difficulty. However, because there is no real teaching or live guidance, you need to be self-disciplined. If you are juggling multiple other courses or responsibilities, it can feel harder to keep up.
comment_workload: The workload is moderate. While the overall content is manageable, some exercises can be time-consuming, especially if you are completely new to programming. If you plan your time well and steadily work through the material, you should be fine.
comment_grading:
This is a pass/fail course. The requirements are:

Submitting at least three out of four exercises and achieving 60% overall for them.

Scoring at least 40% in the final exam.

The final exam is not very difficult, and most students can pass if they’ve done the exercises.

comment_content:
Course contents are given in 5 weeks:
*Lesson 1: Basic Python syntax, variables, strings, lists, boolean operators
*Lesson 2: Function writing, variable scope, for and while loops
*Lesson 3: 2D arrays, print and clear screen, delay function
*Lesson 4: Dictionaries, user input, try-except-else, file I/O
*Lesson 5: Recursion and turtle graphics

When compared with what is taught in COMP1021 (the regular Python introductory course), I think COMP1029P retains most of the contents of COMP1021.

As most Python learners will tell you, Python is actually quite easy to learn. (Professing it is another issue tho) When comparing the difficulties of learning Java in COMP1022P as a newbie at that time (in which I took in Fall Sem in 2021), Python is really much much easier...

And as usual with other programming classes, practice helps, especially considering the fact that COMP1029P is a self-learning course. In general, the course is quite a nice filler course for the long holidays to keep your brain running.
comment_teaching:
Same as what you've seen on the class schedule and others said, this is a self-learning course. Course contents are given on a weekly basis (and you can't access future contents). The emails of both Professor Lam and the TA (Peter Chung) is given for students to ask questions.

Professor Lam gives helpful responses regarding courses, and this even extends to some more out-of-scope contents: for example, he responded to my question why Python file I/O don't work for Microsoft Word--on Sunday, which I didn't expect.

On the other hand, when I asked for a technical problem on one of my assignment, Peter Chung insisted that my code is wrong, and his responses only increased my confusion instead of clarifying my question. What's next? I still get full marks for that assignment the next day.

That said, as I only have 2 occasions to contact the professor and TA with email, my opinions here may not be too representative.
comment_workload:
In general, nearly zero workload (though more workload than spam courses like PHYS1003).

There are a total of 4 assignments since week 2, and you are given with a week to deal with each of them. In fact, they are so easy that I can finish all of them within 2 to 3 days after the assignment is released.

There will also be 2-3 review questions per week, designed to encourage practicing the course content. While all of them won't count towards the final grade (?), review questions for week 2 is a component part of the week's assignment.
comment_grading:
Like other COMP1029* courses, COMP1029P adopts the pass-fail mechanism. You get a pass if you get more than 60% for the mean of all assignments, and 40% for the final exam. For me, I get full mark for all assignments, and scored of 75/100 for the final exam.

The professor also claimed that there should be enough easy questions in the exam, which is true per my experience. At the end at least one guy failed miserably (?*2) at a score of 38 for the exam, showing even those who performed bad in programming may even have a chance to pass this course.

While meaningless to a course which don't pull the curve, most of the homeworks have a mean of over 80%, and the exam's mean is 76.92/100.

comment_content:
A nice introduction to Python. Since Python is dynamically typed, compared to languages like Java and C++, Python is generally more liberal and easier to write. Thus IMHO transitioning from Java/C++ to Python is, in general, easier than vice versa.

Topics included:
1️⃣ Introduction to Python
      🔶 Introduction
      🔶 Variables
      🔶 Strings and Lists
      🔶 Booleans
2️⃣ Essential Programming in Python
      🔶 Functions and Making Decisions
      🔶 Variable Scope
      🔶 For Loops
      🔶 While Loops
3️⃣ 2D Arrays and the Game of Life
      🔶 Two Dimensional Arrays
      🔶 Conway's Game of Life
      🔶 Print, Clear Screen and Delay
4️⃣ Advanced Features
      🔶 Dictionaries
      🔶 User Input and Dealing with Errors
      🔶 File IOs
5️⃣ Recursion and Object-Oriented Programming

      🔶 Turtle Graphics
      🔶 Drawing Trees
      🔶 Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming

The unique features of Python include data structures like lists and dictionaries, and also the way to subset strings and lists using slicing notations. Some elements of Python such as tuples, sets, and lambda functions are not covered, but this is acceptable for an introductory bridging course.
comment_teaching: Just like other COMP1029 bridging courses, there are no face-to-face lectures and all materials are offered online. The content is well structured and clearly delivered, so you should find no problem understanding the course content.
comment_workload: 4 petty assignments and a final exam. Skeleton codes are provided in the assignments and our work was just to fill in a few lines of code. These should be easy jobs for most of us.
comment_grading:
Pass or fail.

Need to get above 60% overall in the assignments (by finishing at least 3 out of 4 of them) and 40% in the final exam. These should be a piece of cake as soon as you pay attention to the deadlines and revise for the exam.

comment_content: The content includes basic data type, arrays and dictionaries, for loop and while loop, input and output handling and basic recursion.
comment_teaching: This is an online course, which means that there is no lecture. You need to learn by yourself by reading though the course material in the CSE website.
comment_workload: Workload is very light. There are four exercises in total, and each takes less than an hour to complete. You only need to complete one exercise every 2-3 weeks.
comment_grading:
This is pass or fail course.
To pass one of these bridging courses, you need to:
obtain 60% or higher of the four exercises total mark, and:
obtain 40% or higher in the final exam
It is extremely easy to get over 40% of grade in the final, so you do not have to worry at all.

comment_content: All the lecture notes are offered online, no attendance is needed. The content is easy to follow for beginner. There is only one final exam and a few assignments. The instructor would offer some  codes for you to finish the assignment, you don't really need to write down a big paragraph of codes to do the assignment. It is more like filling the blank. And the exam questions are only MC and filling the blank questions.
comment_teaching: It is a self-learning course, all the learning are done online, but the lecture notes given by the instructor is complete and useful. I only meet the instructor until the final exam..haha..
comment_workload: As it is a one credit course, the workload is acceptable. For a beginner without any knowledge in python, it might still need time to digest the course material in order to finish the assignment by yourself. In general, the workload is okay for 1 credit course.
comment_grading: This course is pass or  fail course, so the grading is actually not that bad unless you get a F...Unless you didn't read the lecture notes, it is hard to fail the course.
